title: Psalms 11
subtitle: When Foundations Are Destroyed

sections.header: Verses 1–3 — “In the LORD I Take Refuge”

heb.paragraph: "In the LORD I take refuge (ḥāsîṭî). How then can you say to me: “Flee like a bird to your mountain”?" (v.1). David rejects the counsel to flee. His refuge is not geography but God. When others advise escape, faith stands.

refs.note: "God is our refuge... though the earth give way" — the same stand-firm theology.
God as the righteous judge who tests the heart — the same divine examination that Psalm 11:4-5 describes, where the LORD's eyes observe and test humanity from his heavenly throne.
The LORD in his holy temple while all the earth keeps silence — the same cosmic courtroom imagery of divine sovereignty from the heavenly sanctuary.

notes.note: "In the LORD I take refuge." Faith’s first word is not analysis but declaration. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "how then can you say?" is indignant rejection. The advisors mean well; David dismisses them for offering human solutions to a divine problem. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: when foundations crumble, the righteous must remember that God’s throne is unshaken. Calvin: the question is answered not by action but by theology. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: ḥāsîṭî is a completed action — "I HAVE taken refuge." The decision is already made before the psalm begins. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "foundations" are the moral and social order. When institutions collapse, only God’s throne remains. The psalm redirects from horizontal chaos to vertical stability. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the psalm responds to well-meaning but faithless counsel. "Flee" sounds practical; but God is the only real refuge. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the question is rhetorical — it expects no human answer. The righteous can do nothing... except look up (v.4). Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: Advisors tell David to flee — the wicked bend their bows, the foundations are being destroyed (v.3). David’s response: my refuge is the LORD, not the mountains. The question of v.3 is the psalm’s crisis: "When the foundations are being destroyed, what can the righteous do?" The answer is in the second half.

sections.header: Verses 4–7 — “The LORD Is in His Holy Temple”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD is in his holy temple; the LORD is on his heavenly throne (kisʾō)" (v.4). The answer to v.3: God’s throne endures when human foundations collapse. He observes, tests, and judges. The righteous see his face (v.7).

refs.note: "The LORD is in his holy temple; let all the earth be silent" — the same temple-throne confidence.
"There before me was a throne in heaven" — the eternal throne.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "rain fire and brimstone" echoes Sodom’s destruction (Gen 19:24). God’s judgment on the violent follows the Sodom pattern. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: seeing God’s face is the reward that outweighs every trial. Calvin: "in your presence there is fullness of joy" (Ps 16:11). The face of God is the destination of faith. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: the dual location — "holy temple" (earthly) and "heavenly throne" (cosmic) — merges the two. God is present in the temple AND reigning from heaven simultaneously.
The NET: "see his face" uses the verb ḥāzâ (behold, gaze). The vision is sustained, not momentary.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "see his face" is the beatific vision — the goal of all faith. Exile from God’s presence is the worst punishment; seeing his face is the highest reward. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the shift from earth (vv.1–3) to heaven (vv.4–7) reframes the crisis. From below, foundations crumble. From above, the throne stands. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "see his face" echoes the temple pilgrimage tradition. To "see God’s face" was to enter the sanctuary. The psalm promises the permanent version. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: God’s throne is the answer to crumbling foundations. He observes humanity (v.4), examines the righteous (v.5), hates violence (v.5), rains fire on the wicked (v.6). The psalm closes: "the upright will see his face" (v.7) — the ultimate reward: the beatific vision.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God’s throne is the answer to crumbling foundations…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVFor the Lord is righteous, he loves justice; the upright will see his face.ESVFor the LORD is righteous; he loves righteous deeds; the upright shall behold his face.NIVOn the wicked he will rain fiery coals and burning sulfur; a scorching wind will be their lot.ESVLet him rain coals on the wicked; fire and sulfur and a scorching wind shall be the portion of their cup.

rec.text: ""In the LORD I take refuge." Faith’s first word is not analysis but declaration."

rows.label: The Crisis — 11:1–3
rows.text: "When foundations are destroyed, what can the righteous do?" Refuse to flee. Trust God.

hebtext.note: "The LORD is in his holy temple; the LORD is on his heavenly throne (kisʾō)" (v.4). The answer to v.3: God’s throne endures when human foundations collapse. He observes, tests, and judges. The righteous see his face (v.7).

thread.text: "God is our refuge... though the earth give way" — the same stand-firm theology.
"The LORD is in his holy temple; let all the earth be silent" — the same temple-throne confidence.
"There before me was a throne in heaven" — the eternal throne.

themes.note: Faith and Sovereignty peak: when foundations collapse, God’s throne holds. The beatific vision is the ultimate reward.

prayer_prompt: As you consider the question 'When the foundations are being destroyed, what can the righteous do?' consider asking God what remains standing in your life when everything you relied on has been shaken — and whether He is that foundation.
